title: Recycled Fraud Email with Incremental Testing and Geographic Mismatch
narrative: A previously flagged email resurfaces after 60-90 day cooling period, initiating low-value probe transactions ($12-$35) across 3-4 different BINs within 48 hours to test card validity before escalating. The email shows P_emaildomain from free providers (gmail, yahoo) despite R_emaildomain suggesting corporate billing addresses. Pattern emerges when card1_txn_count_1day spikes from 0 to 8+ transactions while maintaining clean payment history on individual cards. Fraudster rotates through digital goods (ProductCD='W') and small physical items to different addr1 values, creating dist1 measurements of 800+ miles between billing and shipping. Transactions cluster in off-peak hours (txn_hour between 2-5 AM local time, is_night=1) when fraud monitoring may be reduced. Email's prior fraud association was 73 days earlier with same behavioral signature: multi-card testing phase followed by attempted bust-out.
indicators: Email domain previously linked to confirmed fraud 60-90 days prior with similar testing behavior
8+ transactions across 3-4 distinct card1 values within 48-hour window, all under $35
dist1 exceeds 800 miles between billing and shipping addresses across multiple orders
P_emaildomain is free provider while R_emaildomain suggests corporate or institutional billing
75%+ of transactions occur during txn_hour 2-5 AM with is_night=1
card1_txn_count_1day jumps from 0 to 8+ while individual cards show no prior fraud flags
ProductCD alternates between 'W' (digital) and 'C' (physical goods) with different addr1 per transaction
summary: An email address that was flagged for fraud 60-90 days ago reappears, immediately running 8+ small transactions ($12-$35) across multiple cards within 48 hours, mostly between 2-5 AM, with billing and shipping addresses over 800 miles apart despite using mismatched free and corporate email domains.
reasoning: The fraudster is testing stolen card numbers with low-value purchases to identify which cards are still active, deliberately spacing the activity to avoid immediate detection, before planning larger fraudulent purchases once valid cards are confirmed.
indicator_explanations: Same email was used in previous fraud with identical small-dollar testing pattern
Email runs micro-transactions across multiple cards to probe for live accounts
Billing and shipping locations are separated by extreme geographic distance
Free personal email conflicts with corporate billing domain suggesting stolen credentials
Activity concentrated in dead-of-night hours when cardholders are asleep
Card suddenly experiences burst activity with zero historical usage baseline
Fraudster alternates digital and physical products while changing delivery addresses each time
